summaries: Between 03:14 and 07:52 UTC on 09 Apr 2026, customers running {service} on Amazon EKS in the us-east-1 Region experienced pod restarts and elevated error rates. Pod {pod} consumed {mem_used} of its {mem_limit} memory limit and was terminated by the OOM killer. The issue affected approximately {error_pct}% of requests during the incident window. The restart counter reached {restart_count} before a fix was deployed at 07:52 UTC.

Tracking ID: AZ-CRX-77031 — Between approximately 01:30 and 06:15 UTC on 11 Apr 2026, a subset of customers using Azure Kubernetes Service in the East US 2 region observed repeated container restarts for workloads running {service}. Heap utilization grew from {start_heap} MB to {heap_mb} MB over a period of {hours} hours before the container was evicted. The issue has been mitigated as of 06:15 UTC.

11:03 AM PDT — We are investigating elevated OOMKilled events for {service} deployments in us-central1. Affected pods show a consistent pattern of memory growth reaching {mem_pct}% of the configured limit ({mem_limit}) prior to termination. The average time between restarts is approximately {hours} hours. Our engineering team is actively investigating.

Google Cloud Status — The issue with Google Kubernetes Engine affecting {service} workloads in us-east4 has been resolved as of 14:20 US/Pacific. Between 08:45 and 14:20 US/Pacific, pods running {service} were repeatedly restarted after heap usage reached {heap_mb} MB. Resident set size grew at a consistent rate independent of incoming request volume throughout the incident window.

Tracking ID: 8_M2-GH7 — Summary of impact: Between approximately 22:10 UTC on 14 Apr and 04:55 UTC on 15 Apr 2026, customers deploying {service} on Azure Container Apps in West Europe experienced container instance recycling. Memory consumption climbed from {start_heap} MB at start to {heap_mb} MB at termination across each {hours}-hour cycle. Root cause analysis is ongoing.

04/09/2026 EKS Pod Restart Incident — Resolved — Between 17:30 and 23:14 PDT, pods running {service} on node {node} in the us-west-2 Region were OOMKilled after consuming {mem_used} against a {mem_limit} limit. AWS CloudWatch metrics confirmed heap growth was independent of request throughput. The restart counter reached {restart_count} before a hotfix was deployed.

Between 06:00 and 11:47 UTC on 07 Apr 2026, a subset of workloads running {service} in Google Cloud region europe-west1 experienced container crashes preceded by steady heap growth. GKE system logs recorded {restart_count} OOMKilled events. GC pause durations grew from a baseline of {gc_baseline} ms to {gc_pause} ms as the heap approached {heap_mb} MB, consistent with garbage collector pressure in the period immediately before each termination.

We experienced elevated container restart rates for {service} in the ap-southeast-1 Region between 13:22 and 18:05 UTC. Pod {pod} was terminated {restart_count} times. Each termination was preceded by a {hours}-hour ramp in resident memory from {start_heap} MB to approximately {mem_used}. The pattern was uniform across all affected pods regardless of traffic volume at the time of each termination.

Tracking ID: AZ-KS9-88214 — Customers using Azure Kubernetes Service in the UK South region reported repeated pod evictions for the {service} workload between 00:45 and 08:30 UTC on 12 Apr 2026. Live heap profiling captured during the incident window showed {object_type} instances accumulating at a rate that did not decrease during low-traffic periods. Mitigation was applied at 08:30 UTC.

05:17 AM PDT — Amazon ECS tasks running {service} in the eu-west-1 Region are being stopped and restarted due to memory limit violations. Container memory reached {mem_used} ({mem_pct}% of the {mem_limit} task limit) before each stop event. The inter-restart interval has been consistent at approximately {hours} hours across {restart_count} observed cycles. We are continuing to investigate.

Google Cloud Status Update — 17:45 US/Pacific — The issue affecting GKE workloads running {service} in us-west1 is ongoing. Since 11:20 US/Pacific, pod {pod} has been restarted {restart_count} times following OOM termination. Heap analysis shows {object_type} objects growing without bound. GC pause time has escalated from {gc_baseline} ms to {gc_pause} ms over the course of each accumulation cycle.

Between 20:00 UTC on 10 Apr and 02:33 UTC on 11 Apr 2026, customers running {service} on Amazon EKS in the ca-central-1 Region experienced degraded availability. The container runtime reported OOMKilled for pod {pod} after memory consumption reached {mem_used} of the {mem_limit} configured limit. The time-to-termination was consistent at {hours} hours across all observed restart cycles.

Tracking ID: GCP-INC-20260408-001 — Between 09:15 and 15:40 UTC on 08 Apr 2026, GKE clusters in asia-northeast1 running {service} experienced repeated pod restarts. Each restart was preceded by a monotonic increase in JVM heap from {start_heap} MB to {heap_mb} MB. GC overhead increased proportionally and reached {gc_pause} ms pause durations in the final minutes before each OOM event. No increase in request rate was observed during the accumulation period.

04/13/2026 Container Restart Incident — Mitigated — Tracking ID: AZ-WE3-20260413 — Between 14:00 and 20:45 UTC, workloads running {service} on Azure Kubernetes Service in the Australia East region experienced {restart_count} container restarts. In each case, memory grew from {start_heap} MB to {mem_used} over approximately {hours} hours before the pod was killed. Object heap profiling identified {object_type} as the dominant growing allocation class.

11:58 PM PDT — We have mitigated the issue affecting {service} pods in the us-east-2 Region. Between 18:15 and 23:58 PDT, pod {pod} was OOMKilled {restart_count} times. Memory consumption followed an identical sawtooth pattern in each cycle, rising from {start_heap} MB at startup to {mem_used} at termination over a period of {hours} hours. The growth rate was not correlated with request throughput.

Between 03:30 and 09:15 UTC on 15 Apr 2026, customers in the Google Cloud europe-west4 region running {service} experienced service interruptions due to repeated pod evictions. The Kubernetes OOM killer terminated pod {pod} after heap reached {heap_mb} MB. GC logs recorded pause events of {gc_pause} ms compared to a pre-incident baseline of {gc_baseline} ms, indicating significant heap pressure in the period preceding each restart.

Tracking ID: AZ-NC7-20260416 — Summary of impact: Between approximately 07:15 and 13:50 UTC on 16 Apr 2026, a subset of Azure Container Instances customers in the Japan East region running {service} experienced container recycling. Memory consumption grew steadily from {start_heap} MB to {mem_used} ({mem_pct}% of the {mem_limit} limit) before each restart. The growth was consistent and independent of inbound request volume during the observation window.

04:22 AM PDT — Amazon EKS pods running {service} in the eu-central-1 Region are experiencing OOMKilled events. Pod {pod} has been restarted {restart_count} times since 01:05 AM PDT. Each termination occurs approximately {hours} hours after the pod starts, after heap grows from {start_heap} MB to {heap_mb} MB. We have identified {object_type} as the primary contributor to heap growth and are working on a resolution.

Google Cloud Status — Resolved — 22:10 US/Pacific — The incident affecting {service} in GKE clusters in northamerica-northeast1 has been resolved. Between 15:45 and 22:10 US/Pacific, pods were terminated {restart_count} times due to memory limit violations. Each termination cycle lasted approximately {hours} hours. Heap profiling showed {object_type} growing without deallocation between garbage collection runs.

Between 11:00 UTC on 06 Apr and 05:30 UTC on 07 Apr 2026, customers running {service} on Amazon EKS in the ap-northeast-1 Region experienced degraded performance due to repeated pod restarts. Pod {pod} was OOMKilled {restart_count} times. In each restart cycle, memory grew from {start_heap} MB at pod start to {mem_used} at termination — a pattern consistent across all {restart_count} events regardless of traffic conditions at the time of each termination.
